item.title: Releasing pbh-rem: A framework for PBH remnants
item.description:
The pbh-rem repository is a Mathematica-based model designed to simulate the cosmological evolution of Primordial Black Holes and the remnants they leave behind.

In standard physics, semi-classical Hawking radiation suggests that black holes evaporate completely. However, mechanisms like Loop Quantum Gravity or the Memory Burden effect introduce a limit to this evaporation, leaving behind stable or very long-lived remnants. To understand the impact of these objects on our universe, we need to carefully track their energy density across all cosmic eras; from radiation to matter and dark energy.

This code provides a rigorous way to do exactly that.

How the framework works

Built for Mathematica 14.1, the pbh-rem pipeline is designed to handle the differential equations that occur around the exact moment a black hole evaporates. The workflow is split into three main parts:

  1. Analytical Derivations (01_alpha.nb): A helper notebook that computes the analytical expressions for the initial abundance coefficients for each species .
  2. The Numerical Engine (02_model.nb): The core solver. It integrates the evolution of the PBH population across cosmic time, computing the transitions between standard evaporation and the stable remnant phase.
  3. Visualization & Data Export (03_visuals.nb): This notebook generates plots of the universe’s history. It also runs an optimization algorithm to find the maximum allowed initial PBH abundance , exporting detailed datasets. For every tested configuration, it tracks 96 different physical variables across four distinct cosmological milestones (Formation, Evaporation, Remnant Diffusion, and Today).

The Paper

This code was developed for our paper: “Signatures of loop quantum gravity in primordial black hole cosmologies” (Antoine Dierckx, Sébastien Clesse, Francesca Vidotto).

You can find the preprint here. Feel free to explore the repository, run the visualizer notebook to see the cosmic history plots, or adapt the model to test your own phenomenological constraints.


View the pbh-rem repository on GitHub →

item.title: Some useful links for physics students
item.dc:creator: Antoine Dierckx
item.description:
Some useful links for physics students

This page brings together some of the resources I have found most useful. The list is personal, but I hope it can also be useful to other students and researchers.

1. Foundations & Lecture Notes

These are some of the best resources I know for learning or revisiting the essentials:

  • David Tong - Lecture Notes: The definitive standard for clear, rigorous introductions to everything from Classical Dynamics to Quantum Field Theory.
  • The Feynman Lectures on Physics: Old but gold.
  • Paul’s Online Math Notes: High school level.
  • MIT OpenCourseWare: Check out the course on general relativity, it’s fantastic.
  • LibreTexts Physics: Peer-authored, open-access textbook content.

2. Advanced Lectures & Seminars

If like me the reviews on arXiv make you a bit anxious, these are a great way to get a more digestible overview of the latest developments in various topics:

  • Perimeter Institute (PIRSA): A massive archive of recorded seminars and specialized courses.
  • Collège de France - Physique et Chimie: High-level academic seminars and colloquia.
  • General Relativity II (Piotr Chruściel): All about black holes.
  • Les Samedis de la Physique (Bruxelles): High-quality lectures from ULB professors.

3. Career Growth: Internships & Research

If you’re looking to do early research or find internships, check out these resources:

  • CERN Summer Student Programme: Particle physics stuff in Geneva.
  • DESY Summer Student Programme: Particle physics stuff in Hamburg.
  • ESA Student Internships: Space science at the European Space Agency.
  • PSI Start (Perimeter Institute): Theoretical physics research at Perimeter Institute in Canada.

4. Free books

All the nice textbooks for free, because knowledge should be free:

  • Sci-Hub: For academic papers.
  • Library Genesis and Z-Library: For books.
